author: @AaronHarper
text: Well said and well produced mate.  I have noticed this trend as well, likening solar (lumping thermal and -voltaic together) to that girl you grew up with.  Every time you have a bad breakup, you rediscover her, but never quite hit the relationship phase. Usually, in the stories where this trope is used, eventually the protagonist will pull his head out of his ass with a resounding pop and marry the girl (Agnes in David Copperfield by Dickens among others), a thing he should have done long ago.  Solar thermal is kinda like that girl in that she's always there for us, waiting patiently for us to realize the happily ever after lies with her.

author: @georgefuters7411
text:
Not forgetting solar photovoltaic thermal PVT panels...more expensive, additional plumbing requirements...but reclaiming 70%of the available solar energy as opposed to 20-25% for PV. Additional advantages: the thermal collector cools the photovoltaic cells and increases their efficiency and the unit does not cease heat production when partially obscured by shadows.
Robert Murray-Smith: you're a true icon and the David Attenborough of engineering 🤗🤗🤗

author: @rhiantaylor3446
text: My brother and his son live in Australia and both have swimming pools they wish to heat for periods of the year. My brother has PV on his roof running a heat pump to warm his pool but his son went for direct Solar heat collection with vacuum tubes. A comparison of the efficiency of the two approaches is interesting. PV captures far less energy but the heat pump  COP gain goes a long way to compensate. The real difference is the cost - I think we should see more use of direct heat capture on UK roofs rather than the dominance of PV - especially now that feed in tariffs are so poor.

author: @matt7403
text: Nice summary!  I find it interesting how much we are currently focused on photovoltaic versus solar thermal. Solar thermal is far more efficient vs solar cells at converting the sun into directly usable energy (as heat). With half of a typical household’s energy use going towards heat, the world ought to move somewhat towards solar thermal, versus the current focus on sunlight to electricity to heat path.

author: @threeMetreJim
text: The phrase "not economically viable" is the reason things get left. Energy suppliers want to maximise profits. Of course it doesn't apply to you so much if you can DIY something at a reasonable cost, but it's still got to be worth it - i.e. payback time relatively short. Solar heating would be great if it was available when needed, but UK winters are often very dull and grey - might save some money on brighter winter days though; heating is a massive use of power in winter.

I think Solar Thermal is the right ways to do Solar Roadways.  

You put some pipes underneath the black tar to carry the heat from the sun sosked roads and store it in thernal batteries lining the roadway.  Collected heat could power road lights at night or maybe give a chargeup to a stalled motorist.

author: @pieterpauwelbeelaerts5995
text: Solar is great. I live under a roof with 18 PV panels. I have free electricity in the day, vasts amounts of it, that is sold for less (allmost nothing), then it becomes night/winter and you need electricity from the grid, pay 3x more. Why? So... I would need to live less in the evening/night but practically, most people work in the day and go home in the evening... when the sun is gone. This makes me want to investigate devices that store kilowatts of energy... but the chemicals are dangerous, like lithium and hydrogen, and they cost far more then a regular solar PV installation... Unless... someone can scale it up, and power a small city with batteries, fuel cells, heat batteries, day and night...

author: @Engineerd3d
text: My father build two large 4x8 foot solar thermal panels for his home. In conjunction with spray foam insulation in his home he can heat about 1000 square foot of his home in the dead of winter without using any gas and very little electric. His panels use twin 120mm silent computer fans to circulate the heat. So 4 in total for under 20w/h of energy to heat up his home. Basically by properly insulating the home and adding thermal heating he cut his fuel bill by at least 80%. Food for thought. Also this is in America where we build with wood and very adapt to using spray foam insulation.

author: @jensodotnet
text: Some months ago I made some calculations, and by my estimate you can store 116,7kW of thermal energy in 1 m3 (0-100*C). If we keep the water between 4 to 90 *C (because it's easy!) it stores about 100kW heat energy, while sand would only store 19,9kW. I also calculated that with average sunshine of 1668 h/year (oslo, norway), with 1000w/m2 solar radiation and with 50% efficient heat collection, you could harvest 834kW(heat)/year, that is per 1 m2 of collector... 🤯🤯 and just thinking about the fact that around ~80% of the electricity bill is used for heating the home(room, water), this is mindboggling, why have it not been done more within this field, or why is it not more popular?! 🤔🤔 EDIT: corrected heat collector efficiency and output..

author: @gee3883
text: South facing glass structures on houses that heat insulated concrete floors are perfect for solar gain/free heat. During the day they collect the heat then the floor radiates it out during the night. Having the house earth integrated aids heat retention and cooling in summer reducing energy. The Earthship design is testimony to that. Love the new vids with the graphics and history lessons RMS. I get the impression there's some 3D printing taking place at the same time your knocking these vids together lol. keep em rollin.

replies.author: @colrodrick8784
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkeringI was just going to make that point.  Of course in Australia you definitely don’t want a south facing house in winter: freezing. I d say that most houses have solar panels these days.  North facing to capture the sun. The entry level size now is 6.6kw (which ours is on a tiny roof).  Most days we generate more than double the power we consume. Of course we have a lot of consumption during the night when there’s no sun.  Need to store it.

author: @peterfelecan3639
text: We are a carbon based civilisation, not only by our chemical structure but also by our main energy conversion method. Of course, all carbon that we use had been stored by using solar based transformation of past living beings. Now is the time to transition our civilisation to direct solar energy conversion or, at least, to use the same carburant as the Sun and build a transitional hydrogen based civilisation based on small scale  cooperation such as it was proposed by Jeremy Rifkin at the beginning of this millennium. What's at the core of the discourse of Robert, as I see it, are serendipitous ways toward that kind of goal.

author: @brotherpaul963
text:
You look like a country gent Rob. Lookin good. I'm very interested in energy and the way it changes form. I'm wondering if you have done any testing on the thermodynamics of graphene sand . Would it improve my pile battery compared to common sand?
I'm always looking for efficiencies to play with. I like to think of the developement of air turbine tech applied to steam and hydro electric systems. Fluid magnetics is interesting stuff.

replies.author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
replies.text: You don't need graphene for it , just ordinary conductive carbon (powder) , for a heat storage. Guy on YT channel , called CAYREX (often mentioned by Robert) did it in one of his videos. Don't know about efficiency , but , in that way you don't need a heating unit (heater) , as the whole container IS the heater (providing that it is a metal container , or you have conductive strip/probe inside the carbon mixed sand). If you use it as chemical energy storage , I wouldn't know , but it would , for sure , reduce internal resistance (I really don't know what the pile battery is).

replies.author: @brotherpaul963
replies.text: @pedjamilosavljevic6235 I'm looking for information comparing the thermodynamic properties of GS compared to regular sand. What are the qualitative changes?

replies.author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
replies.text: @brotherpaul963 In terms of thermodynamic properties , it depends of percentage of graphene (or carbon) in regular send and , of course , of composition of "regular" send , because there are a lot of varieties of sand (silica , granite , sandstone sand , limestone sand , mineral , mixed (impure) types....and/or send particles size). If you have graphene on hand , you can experiment with it , but if not , as I said , ordinary pure carbon will get you comparable results. Carbon and specifically graphene are more associated with use in improving strength and conductivity of materials , less in thermodynamic properties , combined with other materials. I'm sure that someone might have done some research in that (question is , if there is any relation to your particular interest) , but it is hard to answer exactly , without details on a goal you try to achieve and details about the rest of the components. I assume , there will be a lot of calculations involved. BTW , I'm a simple machine engineer , not a chemist , or physicist.

replies.author: @brotherpaul963
replies.text: @pedjamilosavljevic6235 I'm a tinkerer. Rob has a jar of GS. I know that graphene has certain properties of heat flow. I"m wondering if those properties are transfered to the sand.
